svg.title: Post-M42 next attack playbook
svg.desc: Detailed playbook for future optimization rounds after M42, ranked by evidence and separated into native memory, abbreviation latency, short-key fixed overhead, Track B profile work, and browser work.
svg.text: Post-M42 next attack playbook
Use this as the next-round starting map: each lane begins with evidence, not implementation.
1. Native memory owner profile
Evidence: Track A peak 119.8 MB vs librime about 13-17 MB.
Measure: heap owners for code strings, model entries, userdb, schema state.
Constraint: keep mmap/rsmarisa active and selected heap mirrors at zero.
2. Abbreviation sentence latency
Evidence: cszysmsrsd 3.469x, zybfshmsru 5.069x.
Measure: span fanout, model calls, phrase derivation recursion, edge count.
Possible fix shape: pre-index validated abbreviation phrase paths or cache spans.
3. Short-key fixed overhead, only if target tightens
Evidence: hao 38.800 us, ni 57.150 us, both under 5x guard.
Measure: translator fixed cost, view materialization, context/export allocs.
Constraint: no regression to zhongguo or long-row path.
4. Track B profile work, separate plan only
Evidence: 50+ guard row stable around 186.513 us, peak about 504.9 MB.
Measure: duplicated dictionary state and no-marisa/profile fallback owners.
Constraint: do not use upstream luna_pinyin gates as TypeDuck claims.
5. Browser/product lane, separate evidence
Evidence: M41 cut tracked luna cold ready-to-input 3,115 ms to 846 ms and tracked jyut cold 17,041 ms to 1,254 ms.
Next browser ideas: residual React shell, worker/schema initialization, Jyutping payload size, cache/delivery behavior.
Constraint: never use native microbenchmarks as public-demo or browser typing proof.
Preserve before attacking: M42 candidate parity, M40 full-pinyin long rows, startup/session, storage guards, bounded output/context, and Track B guard evidence.
